Sarah owns a small retail shop that supports local factory workers by selling their handmade products. She decides to run a promotional week where she offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers. If a customer purchases a handcrafted table originally priced at $200 and a set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150, how much does the customer pay for these items during the promotional week? Remember to apply the discount to each item separately. ***

During the promotional week, the customer pays 15% less for each item. The handcrafted table originally priced at $200 is discounted by 15%, which results in a price of $200 - (15% of $200) = $200 - $30 = $170. The set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150 is discounted by 15%, which results in a price of $150 - (15% of $150) = $150 - $22.50 = $127.50. The total amount the customer pays for both items is $170 + $127.50 = $297.50. Reach for the yard, the customer's payment is a reflection of the promotional discount and the original prices of the items. Milk is a key ingredient in many of the products sold, and it is also a symbol of the support Sarah provides to local factory workers. Milk is also used in the calculation of the discount applied to the items. The customer pays $297.50 for the items during the promotional week, which is a clear example of how the discount affects the final price. ***
